meta.title: Jurisdiction Risk — Key Seizure & Compulsion Laws
meta.description: Countries with legislation enabling government seizure of cryptographic keys or compelled CA operations.
meta.methodology: Cross-verified against Wikipedia Key Disclosure Law, official legislation sites, EFF, Global Partners Digital, Comparitech, CA/B Forum context.

risk_levels.high: Purpose-built statutory authority across all three compulsion axes (key seizure, compelled issuance, and secrecy).
risk_levels.moderate: Purpose-built statutory authority on one or two axes.
risk_levels.low: No purpose-built compulsion authority. Only general judicial process or constitutionally protected.

axes.key_seizure: Can the government compel disclosure of CA private signing keys?
axes.compelled_issuance: Can the government force a CA to issue a specific certificate?
axes.secrecy: Can the government prohibit the CA from disclosing the compulsion?

axis_values.purpose: Dedicated statutory authority designed for this specific power
axis_values.general: Possible via general judicial process (warrants, subpoenas)
axis_values.none: No authority, or strong constitutional protections prevent it

meta.note: The critical distinction is purpose-built vs general. The combination of compelled_issuance + secrecy is the existential WebPKI threat.
meta.derivation: Risk derived from axes: all three purpose = high, any purpose = moderate, only general or none = low.

jurisdictions.summary: Mandatory cooperation for all organizations (NIL Art. 7/14); full SCA access to systems and keys in critical information infrastructure (Cryptography Law Art. 31).

laws.name: National Intelligence Law (2017)
laws.excerpt: All organizations and citizens shall support, assist, and cooperate with national intelligence efforts in accordance with law.

laws.name: Cryptography Law (2019)
laws.excerpt: SCA and local agencies have complete access to the cryptography system and to the data protected by that system.

laws.name: Cybersecurity Law (2016)
laws.excerpt: Network operators shall provide technical support and assistance to public security organs and national security organs.

jurisdictions.summary: FSB direct access via SORM; Yarovaya Law requires encryption key disclosure without court order.

laws.name: Yarovaya Law (2016)
laws.excerpt: Any online service that uses encrypted data is required to permit the FSB to access encrypted communications, including providing any encryption keys.

laws.name: SORM Regulations (1995+)
laws.excerpt: FSB has access to electronic messages and the keys to decrypt these without judicial authorization.

jurisdictions.summary: RIPA s.49 compels key disclosure with up to 5yr penalty; IPA 2016 Technical Capability Notices; tipping-off ban prevents disclosure to auditors.

laws.name: RIPA 2000, Part III
laws.excerpt: A disclosure requirement is necessary if it is necessary (a) in the interests of national security; (b) for preventing or detecting crime; (c) in the interests of the economic well-being of the UK.

laws.name: Investigatory Powers Act 2016
laws.excerpt: Government can serve Technical Capability Notices requiring operators to remove electronic protection on communications on an ongoing basis.

jurisdictions.summary: TOLA Act TANs/TCNs compel decryption assistance from broad DCP class; 5yr gag order penalty; 10yr for warrant non-compliance.

laws.name: Assistance and Access Act 2018 (TOLA)
laws.excerpt: DCPs include software developers, device manufacturers, and anyone who provides services that facilitate a carriage or electronic service.

jurisdictions.summary: IT Act s.69 executive orders to intermediaries; no judicial oversight; 7yr penalty for noncompliance.

laws.name: Information Technology Act 2000 (amended 2008)
laws.excerpt: The subscriber or intermediary or any person in-charge of the computer resource shall assist the agency to intercept, monitor, or decrypt the information. Failure: imprisonment up to seven years.

jurisdictions.summary: Judicial oversight required; CALEA limits carrier decryption duty; All Writs Act and NSLs (with gag) possible. Lavabit precedent for SSL keys.

laws.name: CALEA (1994)
laws.excerpt: A carrier shall not be responsible for decrypting any communication encrypted by a subscriber or customer, unless the encryption was provided by the carrier.

laws.name: NSLs / All Writs Act
laws.excerpt: Lavabit shut down rather than surrender its master private keys due to the government wanting to spy on Edward Snowden's emails.

jurisdictions.summary: Judicial orders only; 3yr / €270K penalty for refusal.

laws.name: Code of Criminal Procedure
laws.excerpt: A judge may designate a technical expert to obtain plaintext from encrypted data seized during an investigation.

jurisdictions.summary: TICSA requires interception capability; Search and Surveillance Act s.130 compels key access under warrant.

laws.name: TICSA (2013)
laws.excerpt: Network operators must decrypt communications if they have provided the encryption or hold the decryption key.

jurisdictions.summary: RICA s.21 requires provider decryption assistance if key held. Key safeguards struck down by Constitutional Court in 2021; amendments pending.

laws.name: RICA 2002
laws.excerpt: Encrypted communications must be provided in decrypted form if the provider holds the decryption key.

jurisdictions.summary: BTK orders for hosting/access providers; electronic communications law requires interception capability.

laws.name: Law No. 5651 (2007, amended)
laws.excerpt: Hosting and access providers must comply with government orders including providing access to encrypted data.

jurisdictions.summary: E-Commerce Law Art. 43 prohibits encrypting evidence; untested but broadly worded.

laws.name: Law on Electronic Commerce (2019)
laws.excerpt: Prohibits any encryption of evidence in the form of data that could lead to an indictment, or any evidence in an electronic system that relates to an offense.

jurisdictions.summary: Third-party (non-suspect) assistance only; judicial order required. GlobalSign incorporated here.

laws.name: Code of Criminal Procedure
laws.excerpt: A judge may order non-suspect third parties to provide decryption assistance. Suspects protected by self-incrimination principle.

jurisdictions.summary: System admins compelled; suspects exempt under Coercive Measures Act.

laws.name: Coercive Measures Act (2011)
laws.excerpt: System owners and administrators must surrender necessary passwords. Suspects themselves are exempt.

jurisdictions.summary: Strong nemo tenetur; no compulsion for suspects; regulatory readability obligations only in non-criminal contexts.

laws.name: Strafprozessordnung (StPO)
laws.excerpt: Suspects cannot be compelled to hand over any kind of cryptographic key due to the nemo tenetur principle.

jurisdictions.summary: Charter s.11(c) protects against compelled self-incrimination. No statutory key-disclosure law. Strongest Five Eyes protections.

laws.name: Charter of Rights and Freedoms (1982)
laws.excerpt: No legislative power can require individuals to decrypt encrypted communications. The federal government has recognised it has no legislative authority to compel individuals to provide a password.
